title: Filling a deep hole around a wall plug
subtitle: Patch a damaged or oversized fixing hole in plaster or plasterboard so a new plug holds
excerpt: How to fix a wall-fixing hole that has crumbled or been opened too wide so a wall plug will no longer hold. Covers the two-pass build-up in deep-fill filler, the dowel-and-glue method for plasterboard, and re-drilling the pilot hole for the new plug.

glossaryTerms.definition: A powder-based filler designed to set without cracking in cavities up to 20 mm deep. Mixed to a thick custard and pressed into the hole with a filling knife.
A plastic sleeve fitted into a drilled hole that grips a screw as it is driven in. Sized by drill diameter: red plug fits a 5 mm hole, brown plug fits a 6 mm hole, blue plug fits a 10 mm hole.

content.text: A damaged
wall plug

hole will not hold a screw. The plaster has crumbled, the plug spins in place, or the previous owner opened the hole too wide with a hammer. The fix is to fill the hole, let it set hard, and re-drill a fresh pilot.
Step 1, clear the hole

Remove the old plug if it is still in the wall. A bent paper clip hooked behind the plug pulls it out; a small flat screwdriver pries it loose. Brush out loose plaster dust with an old paint brush. Vacuum the hole with the nozzle attachment if possible.
Step 2, choose the right filler
A standard one-coat filler stands in cavities up to 5 mm. A
deep-fill filler

stands in cavities up to 20 mm without cracking. For a wall plug hole, deep-fill is the right choice; the pocket behind the surface is wider than the surface opening, and a thin filler will sink and crack.
Step 3, mix and press in

Mix powder and water to a thick paste; the spec is on the bag. Press the filler into the hole with the 50 mm knife. Push hard so the filler fills the back of the cavity and any side pockets. Smooth the surface flush with the wall.
Step 4, allow to set hard

Most deep-fill fillers set hard in 90 minutes and reach drill-ready strength in 4 hours. Do not drill earlier; the new pilot hole will crack the soft filler. A 24-hour wait is best if the wall will carry a heavy fixing (a shelf bracket, a TV mount).
Step 5, drill the new pilot

Mark the new hole position with the pencil. The new hole can be at the same point as the old one, or shifted by 10 to 15 mm into solid wall if there is space. Drill with the masonry bit sized for the new plug: 5 mm for a red plug, 6 mm for a brown plug. Drill 5 mm deeper than the plug length so the plug seats flush.
Step 6, insert the plug and screw

Tap the new plug into the hole until the rim sits flush with the wall surface. Drive the screw through the bracket or rail and into the plug. The plug expands against the wall as the screw turns. Stop tightening as the bracket pulls firm to the wall; over-tightening pulls the plug out of the wall again.
Variations

On a plasterboard wall where the original fixing was a cavity fixing, deep-fill filler will not hold a fresh fixing on its own. Glue a 50 mm length of 12 mm hardwood dowel into the cavity behind the hole before filling. The dowel becomes the new fixing point and the filler hides the join.
Care

A filled-and-redrilled fixing holds for the life of the wall. If the fixing repeatedly fails over time, the wall material at that point may be soft or damp; consider moving the bracket 50 to 100 mm to a sounder section.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: The filled hole cracks as it dries
attrs.cause: Filler was applied too wet, or in too thick a single pass.
attrs.fix: Scrape out the cracked filler, mix the next batch to a thick paste, and apply in two passes: a base pass to half depth, then a topping pass after the base has set.

attrs.symptom: The new pilot hole crumbles as it is drilled
attrs.cause: The filler had not reached drill-ready hardness, or the drill was used on hammer setting in a soft repair.
attrs.fix: Wait the full 24-hour cure. Use the drill on rotary only (no hammer) on the new pilot through deep-fill filler.
